refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is vague regarding measurement points and conditions. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (full round-trip through sensing, transmission, processing, and actuation), specifies the statistical measure (p95), and anchors to the acceptance criterion. This enables testable verification and clear allocation to design parameters.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events at sensor acquisition, network ingress/egress, operator interface input, command transmission, and actuator response. Collect latency samples across agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Compute p95 latency distribution and validate against 150 ms threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Teleoperation shall acquire sensor data (video, position, force feedback) and transmit to operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: Operator interface shall receive sensor data and present actionable feedback with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator shall input control commands via interface with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation shall transmit operator commands to vehicle and execute with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.5: Teleoperation shall monitor and enforce safety constraints during remote operation with latency ≤ 150 ms (p95) for safety-critical interventions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing, edge computing for sensor preprocessing, and optimized codec selection to minimize transmission and processing delays.
DP-B: Deploy hybrid architecture combining local autonomy (vehicle-side predictive control) with remote supervisory teleoperation, reducing latency sensitivity through predictive buffering.
DP-C: Establish redundant communication paths (primary + failover) with automatic switchover and latency monitoring to ensure p95 compliance under variable network conditions.
DP-D: Implement adaptive bitrate streaming and dynamic sensor sampling rates that adjust to network conditions while maintaining latency bounds through quality-of-service negotiation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-C (Integrated): Implement a dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing and edge preprocessing (DP-A) combined with redundant communication paths and automatic failover (DP-C) to ensure p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions. This addresses both the nominal path optimization and resilience to network variability.

potential_sources: Coupling between network stack optimization (DP-A) and safety constraint enforcement (FR-NGHE-008.5): safety monitoring must not introduce additional latency. Mitigation: implement safety checks in parallel with data transmission, not serially.

Coupling between redundant paths (DP-C) and operator interface consistency: failover switching may cause transient state inconsistency. Mitigation: implement sequence numbering and state reconciliation in operator interface.

Coupling between edge preprocessing (DP-A) and vehicle autonomy: preprocessing decisions must not conflict with safety policies. Mitigation: safety policies must be enforced before preprocessing decisions.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP combination is decoupled from FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4 (latency budgeting) but introduces coupling risk with FR-NGHE-008.5 (safety enforcement). Recommend explicit safety-latency co-design review.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are well-characterized and stable within agreed profiles (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). RISK: If actual network conditions exceed agreed profiles, p95 latency may be violated. Mitigation: implement real-time network monitoring and graceful degradation.

ASSUMPTION: Edge computing resources (vehicle-side preprocessing) are available with sufficient compute capacity. RISK: If vehicle compute is constrained, preprocessing may introduce latency instead of reducing it. Mitigation: profile vehicle compute and validate preprocessing latency empirically.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant communication paths can be provisioned and maintained (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP). RISK: Cost and operational complexity may be prohibitive. Mitigation: evaluate cost-benefit trade-off with stakeholders.

ASSUMPTION: Operator interface can tolerate brief failover transients (< 500 ms). RISK: If operator is in critical control action, failover may cause safety incident. Mitigation: implement predictive failover and operator notification.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify latency budgets for each subsystem (sensor acquisition ≤ 20 ms, network transmission ≤ 50 ms, edge processing ≤ 20 ms, operator interface ≤ 25 ms, command transmission ≤ 25 ms, vehicle actuation ≤ 10 ms) to enable allocation and verification. Refine DP-C to specify failover detection time (< 50 ms) and state reconciliation protocol.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication path including sensor data acquisition and transmission, operator interface, command reception and transmission, and latency monitoring. Responsible for achieving p95 latency ≤ 150 ms through network stack optimization, edge preprocessing, redundant paths, and failover management.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor telemetry (video, position, force feedback, diagnostics) to operator interface and monitoring systems. Latency-critical: must be transmitted with minimal delay.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from operator interface. Latency-critical: must be received and executed with minimal delay.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module exchanges safety constraints and alerts with Safety Services. Receives safety policies and constraints; sends safety-critical alerts and intervention requests. Must enforce safety constraints within latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for all remote communication (sensor transmission, command reception, failover switching). Responsible for optimizing latency through priority queuing, redundancy, and adaptive quality-of-service.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own teleoperation implementation details. Teleoperation is a major subsystem that should be owned by a dedicated module. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure latency accountability.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side hardware and local control. Teleoperation latency depends on vehicle-side sensor acquisition and actuation, but the end-to-end latency optimization (network stack, edge preprocessing, redundancy) is not primarily a vehicle concern. Assigning to Vehicle would diffuse network and operator interface responsibilities.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the teleoperation communication path, latency optimization, and failover management. It owns the network stack, edge preprocessing, redundant paths, and operator interface integration. This assignment is highly cohesive and minimizes diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but not the teleoperation communication path or latency optimization. Assigning latency to Safety Services would diffuse responsibility and create coupling between safety and network concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-008 because it encapsulates the end-to-end communication path, network optimization, and latency monitoring. The module has clear boundaries (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator), well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link), and direct control over latency-critical subsystems (network stack, edge preprocessing, redundancy).
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may not own vehicle-side sensor acquisition hardware (e.g., camera, IMU). If Vehicle module owns sensors, latency accountability is split. MITIGATION: Clarify that Teleoperation owns sensor acquisition software and timing, while Vehicle owns sensor hardware. Establish interface contract for sensor data latency.

RISK: Teleoperation module may not own operator interface hardware (e.g., display, input devices). If a separate Operator Interface module exists, latency accountability is split. MITIGATION: Clarify that Teleoperation owns operator interface software and timing, while Operator Interface module owns hardware. Establish interface contract for interface latency.

RISK: Teleoperation module may not own Network Link infrastructure (e.g., routers, gateways). If Network Link is owned by a separate Infrastructure module, latency optimization may be constrained. MITIGATION: Establish service-level agreement with Network Link owner specifying latency bounds and priority queuing requirements.

RISK: Safety Services may impose safety constraints that conflict with latency optimization (e.g., additional safety checks that increase latency). MITIGATION: Co-design safety and latency requirements; ensure safety checks are parallelized with latency-critical operations.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle (sensor data latency ≤ 20 ms), Teleoperation and Operator Interface (interface latency ≤ 25 ms), and Teleoperation and Network Link (transmission latency ≤ 50 ms). Define latency monitoring and reporting mechanisms within Teleoperation module to enable real-time verification and failover triggering.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly specifies the latency requirement (p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end) and the selected DP (low-latency network stack + redundant paths) directly addresses this requirement. The DP is logically sound and feasible. The coupling check identified potential safety-latency interactions but these are manageable through co-design. The FR-DP mapping is decoupled and non-redundant.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle module on sensor acquisition latency. Recommend explicit interface contract specifying sensor data latency budget (≤ 20 ms).
Teleoperation module must coordinate with Safety Services on safety constraint enforcement latency. Recommend parallel execution of safety checks to avoid serial latency accumulation.
Teleoperation module must coordinate with Network Link on transmission latency and priority queuing. Recommend service-level agreement specifying network latency bounds.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05) for the selected DP. The module owns the end-to-end communication path and latency optimization. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. Ownership is clear and accountability is concentrated.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is testable and verifiable. Latency can be measured end-to-end using timestamped events at sensor acquisition, network ingress/egress, operator interface, and actuator response. The p95 metric is statistically sound and can be computed from latency samples. Verification can be performed in lab and field environments under agreed network conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of CR-NGHE-008 to the Teleoperation module is clear and unambiguous. The module owns the latency-critical path and has direct control over optimization mechanisms (network stack, edge preprocessing, redundancy). Sub-FRs (008.1 through 008.5) can be allocated to specific subsystems within Teleoperation (sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator interface, command execution, safety monitoring).

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit latency budgets for each sub-FR (008.1 through 008.5) and allocate to specific subsystems within Teleoperation. Ensure budgets sum to ≤ 150 ms with margin for variability.
Define latency monitoring and reporting mechanisms within Teleoperation module. Implement real-time latency tracking and alerting to enable proactive failover and graceful degradation.

Establish interface contracts with Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link specifying latency bounds and quality-of-service requirements. Document assumptions about network conditions and vehicle compute capacity.
Conduct safety-latency co-design review with Safety Manager and System Engineer to ensure safety constraints do not introduce unacceptable latency and latency optimization does not compromise safety.

decision.justification: The refined FR is clear and testable, the selected DP is logically sound and feasible, and the assigned IM (Teleoperation) has high containment and low diffusion. However, the FR is not atomic and has been decomposed into five sub-FRs (008.1 through 008.5) that address different aspects of the teleoperation path (sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator interface, command execution, safety monitoring). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to allocate to specific subsystems and design parameters. Additionally, the selected DP (low-latency network stack + redundant paths) is a high-level concept that requires further refinement into specific design choices (codec selection, edge preprocessing algorithms, failover detection mechanisms, etc.). Recommend proceeding to the next decomposition level to refine sub-FRs and DPs.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified several coordination risks (sensor acquisition latency, safety constraint enforcement, network transmission latency) that require explicit interface contracts and co-design. These risks are manageable but require further analysis at the next decomposition level. The recommended revisions (latency budgets, monitoring mechanisms, interface contracts, safety-latency co-design) are best addressed through sub-FR decomposition and detailed DP refinement.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Teleoperation shall acquire sensor data (video, position, force feedback) and transmit to operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: Operator interface shall receive sensor data and present actionable feedback with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator shall input control commands via interface with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation shall transmit operator commands to vehicle and execute with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.5: Teleoperation shall monitor and enforce safety constraints during remote operation with latency ≤ 150 ms (p95) for safety-critical interventions.

assumptions: Network conditions are well-characterized and stable within agreed profiles (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Edge computing resources (vehicle-side preprocessing) are available with sufficient compute capacity.
Redundant communication paths can be provisioned and maintained (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP).
Operator interface can tolerate brief failover transients (< 500 ms).
Safety constraints can be enforced in parallel with latency-critical operations without introducing serial latency accumulation.
Vehicle module owns sensor hardware and provides sensor data interface with latency ≤ 20 ms.
Operator Interface module owns display and input hardware and provides interface latency ≤ 25 ms.
Network Link module provides transmission latency ≤ 50 ms under agreed network conditions.
open_risks: Safety-latency coupling: Safety constraints may introduce latency that violates p95 requirement. Mitigation: co-design safety and latency; parallelize safety checks.
Network variability: Actual network conditions may exceed agreed profiles. Mitigation: implement real-time monitoring and graceful degradation.
Vehicle compute constraints: Edge preprocessing may introduce latency instead of reducing it. Mitigation: profile vehicle compute and validate preprocessing latency empirically.
Operator interface consistency: Failover switching may cause transient state inconsistency. Mitigation: implement sequence numbering and state reconciliation.
Cost and operational complexity: Redundant communication paths may be prohibitive. Mitigation: evaluate cost-benefit trade-off with stakeholders.
evidence_needed: Network characterization: Bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles under agreed conditions.
Vehicle compute profile: Available compute capacity and preprocessing latency measurements.
Operator interface latency: Measured latency for sensor data presentation and command input.
Safety constraint latency: Measured latency for safety checks and constraint enforcement.
Failover detection time: Measured time to detect network failure and switch to redundant path.
End-to-end latency measurements: Timestamped events at sensor acquisition, network ingress/egress, operator interface, and actuator response under agreed network conditions.
Safety-latency co-design review: Documentation of safety constraints and latency optimization trade-offs.
